topic: Sponsor / sponsor-investigator clinical-trial responsibilities for an early-phase IND study (21 CFR Part 312 Subpart D + safety/annual reporting + ICH E6(R3) Section 5) - regulatory reference for OSSICRO
summary: Under 21 CFR Part 312 the sponsor is the party who takes responsibility for and initiates a clinical investigation and holds the IND; Subpart D (312.50-312.59) enumerates the sponsor's affirmative duties, and Subpart B (312.32-312.33) governs safety and annual reporting to FDA. A sponsor may transfer any or all obligations to a Contract Research Organization only in a signed writing (312.52); an obligation not described in that writing is deemed not transferred, and a CRO that assumes an obligation becomes subject to the same regulatory action as a sponsor - meaning the transferee must be a legal person/entity, so an open-source system such as OSSICRO is a tool that supports compliance, not a lawful transferee of obligations. A physician who both initiates and conducts the trial is a 'sponsor-investigator' (312.3(b)) and simultaneously bears the full sponsor obligation set and the full investigator obligation set, which cannot be delegated away to himself or to software. ICH E6(R3) Section 5 (FDA final guidance, September 2025) layers a quality-by-design and risk-based quality-management framework over the CFR: proportionate risk management, documented oversight of service providers, risk-based monitoring, safety assessment, investigational-product control, and data governance with ALCOA+ data integrity and a complete Trial Master File / essential records (Appendix 2). Regardless of delegation, ultimate accountability for participant safety, data reliability, and the FDA relationship (the IND, safety reports, annual reports) remains with the sponsor/sponsor-investigator.

obligations_or_content.detail: Select qualified investigators; provide them the information needed to conduct the investigation properly; ensure proper monitoring; ensure the investigation is conducted per the general investigational plan and protocols in the IND; maintain an effective IND; and ensure FDA and all participating investigators are promptly informed of significant new adverse effects or risks with respect to the drug.

An individual who both initiates and conducts an investigation, and under whose immediate direction the drug is administered/dispensed, is a 'sponsor-investigator' and must satisfy BOTH the sponsor obligations and the investigator obligations of Part 312. The role cannot be assigned to a non-individual (no entity, no software) and the sponsor duties cannot be self-delegated.

obligations_or_content.item: Transfer of obligations to a CRO - in writing
obligations_or_content.detail: Any transfer of sponsor obligations to a CRO must be described in writing; a partial transfer must describe each obligation assumed, and a full transfer may use a general statement. Any obligation not covered by the written description is deemed not transferred; a CRO that assumes an obligation must comply with the applicable regulations and is subject to the same regulatory action as a sponsor. Because the transferee must be a person/legal entity, software (OSSICRO) cannot be a transferee.
Select only investigators qualified by training and experience as appropriate experts to investigate the drug.
Ship investigational new drug only to investigators participating in the investigation.

obligations_or_content.item: Obtaining the investigator's commitment package (Form FDA 1572)
obligations_or_content.detail: Before permitting an investigator to begin, obtain a signed Form FDA 1572 with the investigator's identity, education/training, protocol facilities/IRB, and commitments to conduct per protocol, obtain informed consent (Part 50), comply with reporting per 312.64, and ensure IRB review (Part 56).
Obtain a curriculum vitae or other statement of qualifications showing education, training, and experience qualifying the investigator as an expert in the clinical investigation.

Obtain sufficient financial-interest information to allow the sponsor to submit complete and accurate certification or disclosure statements required under Part 54, plus the investigator's commitment to update that information for one year after study completion.
Select a monitor qualified by training and experience to monitor the progress of the investigation.

For investigations involving an exception from informed consent (50.24) or emergency research, monitor progress, promptly submit required public-disclosure copies to the IND file and FDA Dockets, and, if an IRB determines the study cannot be approved, promptly notify FDA, the reviewing IRBs, and investigators of the same or substantially equivalent study.
Before the investigation begins, give each participating clinical investigator an investigator brochure containing the information described in 312.23(a)(5).

obligations_or_content.item: Keeping investigators informed of new safety findings
obligations_or_content.detail: Keep each participating investigator informed of new observations discovered by or reported to the sponsor, particularly adverse effects and safe use, through updated brochures, reprints, letters, or other means, including safety information required under 312.32.

obligations_or_content.item: Monitoring the progress of all investigations
obligations_or_content.detail: Monitor the progress of all clinical investigations being conducted under the IND.

obligations_or_content.item: Reviewing safety/effectiveness evidence and securing compliance
obligations_or_content.detail: Review and evaluate the evidence of safety and effectiveness as obtained from the investigator; if an investigator fails to comply with the signed agreement (1572), the general investigational plan, or applicable regulations, promptly secure compliance or discontinue shipments and end the investigator's participation, require return/disposition of drug per 312.59, and notify FDA.

obligations_or_content.item: Discontinuation of investigations presenting unreasonable risk
obligations_or_content.detail: If the drug is determined to present an unreasonable and significant risk to subjects, discontinue the investigations that present the risk as soon as possible and no later than 5 working days after the determination; notify FDA, all IRBs, and all investigators; assure disposition of outstanding drug supplies; and furnish FDA a full report.

obligations_or_content.item: Recordkeeping - drug disposition and financial interests
obligations_or_content.detail: Maintain adequate records of receipt, shipment, and disposition of the investigational drug (investigator name, date, quantity, and batch/code mark), and complete and accurate records of any financial interests paid to investigators and other Part 54 financial interests.

Retain the required records and reports for 2 years after a marketing application is approved for the drug, or, if no application is filed or approved, 2 years after shipment and delivery of the drug for investigational use is discontinued and FDA has been so notified.

obligations_or_content.item: Permitting FDA inspection of sponsor records
obligations_or_content.detail: Upon request of a properly authorized FDA officer or employee, at reasonable times, permit access to, and copying and verification of, any records and reports relating to a clinical investigation; controlled-substance records must additionally be available to DEA and stored securely.

obligations_or_content.item: Disposition of unused investigational drug supply
obligations_or_content.detail: Assure the return of all unused supplies of the investigational drug from each investigator whose participation is discontinued or terminated (or authorize alternative disposition that does not expose humans to risk), and maintain written records of any disposition per 312.57.

Notify FDA and all participating investigators in an IND safety report of any suspected adverse reaction that is both serious and unexpected (reasonable possibility the drug caused it), and of findings from clinical/epidemiologic studies, pooled analyses, animal or in vitro testing, or a clinically important increased rate of occurrence, as soon as possible but no later than 15 calendar days.

Notify FDA of any unexpected fatal or life-threatening suspected adverse reaction as soon as possible but no later than 7 calendar days after initial receipt of the information (by telephone, facsimile, or other means), with a complete written follow-up report as a 15-day IND safety report.

Promptly review all information relevant to the safety of the drug from any source, foreign or domestic, including clinical or epidemiological investigations, reports in the scientific literature, animal or in vitro studies, and reports from foreign regulators or other manufacturers.

Within 60 days of the anniversary date the IND went into effect, submit a brief report of the progress of the investigation, including status of each study, summary safety information (most frequent/serious adverse experiences, deaths, subject dropouts, IND safety reports), the general investigational plan for the coming year, investigator-brochure revisions, significant Phase 1 protocol modifications not previously reported, and relevant foreign marketing developments.

obligations_or_content.item: ICH E6(R3) - quality by design and risk-based quality management
obligations_or_content.detail: Build quality into trial design; identify factors critical to quality and the risks that threaten them; and manage risk proportionate to the importance of the data and the risks to participant safety and result reliability (avoiding disproportionate processes and non-critical data collection).
obligations_or_content.citation: ICH E6(R3) Section 5 (Quality Management; Risk-Based Quality Management); FDA final guidance Sept 2025

obligations_or_content.item: ICH E6(R3) - oversight of service providers
obligations_or_content.detail: The sponsor retains responsibility for and must maintain documented oversight of any trial-related duties and functions delegated to a service provider (including CROs and technology vendors); delegation does not transfer accountability for the quality and integrity of the trial.
obligations_or_content.citation: ICH E6(R3) Section 5 (Oversight and Service Providers)

obligations_or_content.detail: Develop a systematic, prioritized, risk-based monitoring strategy (which may combine centralized and on-site monitoring) to verify participant protection, protocol compliance, and reliability of reported data.

obligations_or_content.item: ICH E6(R3) - safety assessment and reporting / pharmacovigilance oversight
obligations_or_content.detail: Maintain ongoing safety evaluation of the investigational product, ensure expedited and periodic safety reporting to regulators, IRB/IECs, and investigators, and keep the investigator brochure current as the safety profile evolves.
obligations_or_content.citation: ICH E6(R3) Section 5 (Safety Assessment and Reporting)

obligations_or_content.detail: Ensure the investigational product is manufactured, labeled, coded, supplied, and accounted for appropriately, with documented handling, storage, and reconciliation.

obligations_or_content.item: ICH E6(R3) - data governance, ALCOA+, and essential records/TMF
obligations_or_content.detail: Ensure data integrity across the data lifecycle - records that are Attributable, Legible, Contemporaneous, Original, Accurate, plus Complete, Consistent, Enduring, and Available (ALCOA+) - with validated systems, audit trails, and traceability; and maintain the essential records (Trial Master File) that permit reconstruction and evaluation of trial conduct.
obligations_or_content.citation: ICH E6(R3) Section 5 (Data Governance and Recordkeeping) and Appendix 2 (Essential Records); FDA data-integrity guidance

key_documents: IND application (Form FDA 1571) and the general investigational plan/protocol
Clinical trial protocol and protocol amendments
Investigator curriculum vitae / statement of qualifications
Financial disclosure documents (Form FDA 3454 certification / 3455 disclosure; Part 54)
Written transfer-of-obligations agreement with any CRO (312.52)
IND safety reports (7-day and 15-day; MedWatch Form FDA 3500A or narrative)
Monitoring plan / risk-based monitoring strategy and monitoring reports
Drug accountability, shipment, and disposition records (312.57, 312.59)
Trial Master File / essential records set (ICH E6(R3) Appendix 2)
Quality-by-design / risk-based quality management plan (ICH E6(R3) Section 5)
Informed consent form and IRB approval documentation (Parts 50 and 56)

regulatory_citations.title: Definitions - sponsor, investigator, sponsor-investigator
regulatory_citations.relevance: Establishes that a sponsor-investigator carries both sponsor and investigator obligation sets; the core framing for a physician running an early-phase trial as sponsor-investigator.

regulatory_citations.title: General responsibilities of sponsors
regulatory_citations.relevance: Umbrella duties: qualified investigators, information provision, monitoring, protocol adherence, effective IND, prompt safety communication.

regulatory_citations.title: Transfer of obligations to a contract research organization
regulatory_citations.relevance: Governs written CRO transfer; undocumented obligations stay with sponsor; CRO subject to same regulatory action; only a person/entity can be a transferee (software cannot).

regulatory_citations.title: Selecting investigators and monitors
regulatory_citations.relevance: Qualification of investigators/monitors; shipment control; collection of 1572, CV, and financial disclosure.

regulatory_citations.title: Emergency research under 50.23 and 50.24
regulatory_citations.relevance: Special-protocol / exception-from-informed-consent oversight and public-disclosure duties.

regulatory_citations.title: Informing investigators
regulatory_citations.relevance: Investigator brochure before start; ongoing duty to inform investigators of new safety observations.

regulatory_citations.title: Review of ongoing investigations
regulatory_citations.relevance: Monitoring, safety/effectiveness review, securing compliance or discontinuing non-compliant investigators, and 5-working-day discontinuation for unreasonable risk.

regulatory_citations.title: Recordkeeping and record retention
regulatory_citations.relevance: Drug disposition and financial-interest records; 2-year retention rule.

regulatory_citations.title: Inspection of sponsor's records and reports
regulatory_citations.relevance: FDA (and DEA for controlled substances) access, copying, and verification of sponsor records.

regulatory_citations.title: Disposition of unused supply of investigational drug
regulatory_citations.relevance: Return/alternative disposition of unused drug from discontinued investigators; written disposition records.

regulatory_citations.title: IND safety reporting
regulatory_citations.relevance: Definitions (serious, unexpected, life-threatening, suspected adverse reaction) and the 15-day and 7-day reporting timelines to FDA and investigators.

regulatory_citations.title: Annual reports
regulatory_citations.relevance: 60-day annual IND progress report and its required contents.

regulatory_citations.title: Financial disclosure by clinical investigators
regulatory_citations.relevance: The financial-interest information the sponsor must collect and certify/disclose to FDA.

regulatory_citations.title: Protection of human subjects; Institutional Review Boards
regulatory_citations.relevance: Informed consent and IRB requirements the sponsor must ensure investigators satisfy.

regulatory_citations.title: Investigator brochure content
regulatory_citations.relevance: Defines the brochure the sponsor must furnish under 312.55(a).

regulatory_citations.title: Good Clinical Practice - Sponsor
regulatory_citations.relevance: Modern GCP sponsor framework: quality management, risk-based quality management, oversight of service providers, monitoring, safety assessment/reporting, investigational product, data governance and recordkeeping.

regulatory_citations.title: Essential Records for the Conduct of a Clinical Trial
regulatory_citations.relevance: The Trial Master File / essential-records set the sponsor must maintain to permit reconstruction and evaluation of the trial.

non_delegable_or_gating: Ultimate accountability for participant safety and the quality/integrity/reliability of trial data - retained by the sponsor even when duties are transferred to a CRO or supported by software (21 CFR 312.52; ICH E6(R3) Section 5 oversight).
The CRO transferee must be a legal person or entity; an open-source system (OSSICRO) is a compliance tool, not a lawful transferee of any 312.52 obligation, and cannot 'assume' a sponsor obligation.
A sponsor-investigator holds BOTH the sponsor and the investigator obligation sets (21 CFR 312.3(b)) and cannot self-delegate the sponsor duties away.

Holding and maintaining an effective IND and the direct regulatory relationship with FDA (submissions, safety reports, annual reports) - the IND belongs to the sponsor (21 CFR 312.50, 312.32, 312.33).
The decision to discontinue an investigation presenting an unreasonable and significant risk to subjects within 5 working days, with notifications to FDA/IRBs/investigators (21 CFR 312.56(d)).
The judgment to discontinue or terminate a non-compliant investigator and to secure compliance (21 CFR 312.56(b)) - execution can be delegated; the accountability cannot.

Review of incoming safety information and the causality/expectedness/seriousness determinations that trigger 7-day and 15-day IND safety reports - a PV vendor may draft, but the sponsor owns the assessment and the report (21 CFR 312.32).
Certification/disclosure of clinical-investigator financial interests to FDA (21 CFR Part 54; 312.53(c)(4)).
Signing/attesting to regulatory submissions and permitting FDA inspection of sponsor records (21 CFR 312.58).

sources.name: 21 CFR 312.50 - General responsibilities of sponsors (Cornell LII, mirrors eCFR)
21 CFR 312.52 - Transfer of obligations to a CRO
